annotations.label: Euclidean distance between two points

anchor.snippet: the length of a straight line between them

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq states dist x y = √(∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2), the Euclidean distance between two points.

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in the plane

anchor.snippet: in the plane is given by

annotations.note: The planar formula √((x₂-x₁)²+(y₂-y₁)²) is the n=2 case of the general EuclideanSpace.dist_eq.

anchors.snippet: in the plane is given by

annotations.label: Euclidean distance in three-dimensional space

anchor.snippet: in three-dimensional space, the distance between them is

annotations.note: The 3-D distance formula is the n=3 case of the general EuclideanSpace.dist_eq.

anchors.snippet: in three-dimensional space, the distance between them is

annotations.label: Great-circle distance

anchor.section: Shortest-path distance on a curved surface
anchor.snippet: approximated mathematically by the great-circle distance on a sphere

annotations.note: No great-circle / spherical distance definition exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geodesic

anchor.section: Shortest-path distance on a curved surface
anchor.snippet: is known as a geodesic

annotations.note: Only incidental docstring mentions of 'geodesic' appear; there is no general geodesic/shortest-path-on-a-manifold definition.

annotations.label: Manhattan distance

anchor.snippet: is given by the Manhattan distance

annotations.note: PiLp.dist_eq_of_L1 gives dist x y = ∑ i, dist (x i) (y i), the L¹/Manhattan distance, on the general Pi type.

annotations.label: Chebyshev distance

anchor.snippet: is the minimum number of moves a king must make on a chessboard

annotations.note: dist_pi_def defines the sup (L∞) metric dist f g = sup over coordinates of nndist, which is the Chebyshev distance.

annotations.label: Statistical distances

anchor.snippet: statistical distances measure the degree of difference between two probability distributions

annotations.note: No general 'statistical distance' notion is defined, though specific divergences like klDiv exist as instances.

annotations.label: Squared Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: The most elementary is the squared Euclidean distance

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_sq_eq states dist x y ^ 2 = ∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2, the squared Euclidean distance.

annotations.label: Relative entropy (Kullback–Leibler divergence)

anchor.snippet: The most important in information theory is the relative entropy

annotations.note: klDiv μ ν is the Kullback–Leibler divergence (relative entropy) between two measures.

annotations.label: Mahalanobis and energy distances

anchor.snippet: Other important statistical distances include the Mahalanobis distance and the energy distance

annotations.note: Neither the Mahalanobis distance nor the energy distance is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Edit distance / string metric

anchor.snippet: between two strings measures how different they are

annotations.note: No edit/Levenshtein distance or string metric is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Edit distance of dog/dot/cat

anchor.snippet: which differ by just one letter, are closer than

annotations.note: This illustrative example has no formalization, since edit distance itself is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distance between vertices in a graph

anchor.snippet: measured by the length of the shortest edge path between them

annotations.note: SimpleGraph.dist is the length of the shortest walk between two vertices (dist_eq_sInf over walk lengths).

annotations.label: Six degrees of separation

anchor.snippet: the distance between any two vertices is at most six

annotations.note: This sociological example/conjecture has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Metric / distance function

anchor.snippet: A metric or distance function is a function d which takes pairs of points or objects to real numbers

annotations.note: MetricSpace (built on PseudoMetricSpace's dist_self, dist_comm, dist_triangle plus eq_of_dist_eq_zero) is exactly the metric/distance-function axiomatization.

anchors.snippet: A metric or distance function is a function d which takes pairs of points or objects to real numbers

annotations.label: Distance between multi-point objects

anchor.snippet: measuring the physical distance between objects that consist of more than one point

annotations.note: Metric.infDist gives point-to-set distance, related infrastructure for distance between extended objects, but no general object-to-object distance is named.

annotations.label: Distance between sets

anchor.snippet: is the infimum of the distances between any two of their respective points

annotations.note: Mathlib defines point-to-set infimum distance (Metric.infDist) but no dedicated set-to-set infimum-distance function.

anchors.snippet: is the infimum of the distances between any two of their respective points

annotations.label: Set distance is not a metric

anchor.snippet: This does not define a metric on the set of such subsets

annotations.note: There is no theorem stating the inf set-distance fails the metric axioms.

anchors.snippet: This does not define a metric on the set of such subsets

annotations.label: Hausdorff distance

anchor.snippet: can be thought of as measuring how far they are from perfectly overlapping

annotations.note: Metric.hausdorffDist defines the Hausdorff distance between two sets.

annotations.label: Hausdorff distance is a metric on compact subsets

anchor.snippet: defines a metric on the set of compact subsets of a metric space

annotations.note: NonemptyCompacts.instMetricSpace gives a MetricSpace structure on nonempty compact subsets whose dist is the Hausdorff distance (NonemptyCompacts.dist_eq).

annotations.label: Distance travelled (arc length)

anchor.snippet: is the length of a specific path travelled between two points

annotations.note: eVariationOn is the total variation (supremum of summed segment distances), which is the arc length / distance travelled along a path.

annotations.label: Signed distance travelled

anchor.snippet: The distance travelled may also be signed

annotations.note: variationOnFromTo is a signed variation (sign from endpoint order), close to but not the physics notion of signed distance travelled along a reversing path.

annotations.label: Circular distance

anchor.snippet: Circular distance is the distance traveled by a point on the circumference of a wheel

annotations.note: No 'circular distance' (wheel-circumference travel) notion is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Displacement

anchor.snippet: measures the change in position of an object during an interval of time

annotations.note: Affine point subtraction (b -ᵥ a via VSub) gives a displacement vector, but no named physics 'displacement' concept exists.

annotations.label: Directed distance

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called the directed distance

annotations.note: The directed distance from a to b is captured by the affine subtraction b -ᵥ a (VSub), but is not itself a named definition.

annotations.label: Signed distance function

anchor.snippet: is the orthogonal distance of a given point x to the boundary of a set Ω

annotations.note: AffineSubspace.signedInfDist gives signed orthogonal distance to an affine subspace, a special case of the general signed distance function to a set's boundary.
